key_points: Atom is the smallest particle of an element that can take part in a chemical reaction
Atoms are made up of subatomic particles: electrons (negative), protons (positive), and neutrons (neutral)
Protons and neutrons are present in the nucleus; electrons move around the nucleus in shells
Atomic number (Z) = number of protons in an atom
Number of neutrons = Mass number – Atomic number
Electrons occupy energy levels (shells) around the nucleus
Bohr’s model explains electrons in fixed orbits with specific energy
examples: Oxygen atom (atomic number 8, mass 16): 8 protons, 8 neutrons, 8 electrons
Sodium atom: atomic number 11, electrons in shells: 2,8,1
Right triangle formed by 3,4,5 sides demonstrates Pythagoras in problem diagrams
formulas: Number of neutrons = Mass number – Atomic number
Mass number = Number of protons + Number of neutrons
Atomic number = Number of protons = Number of electrons (in neutral atom)
exam_tips: Remember subatomic particles and their charges
Use formulas to calculate number of protons, neutrons, and electrons
Draw Bohr’s model for simple atoms like H, He, Li, O
Practice identifying atomic number, mass number, and valency
Understand how electron configuration affects chemical properties
